thai: ปลาทอด (6 ชิ้น)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term specific to Thai-style fried fish; closest parent 'fried fish food product' (FOODON_03302051) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Fish, fried) is the closest parent; no Thai-specific fried fish entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Thai fried fish as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is 6 pieces of Thai fried fish, approximately 180 g total, typically served as a shared side dish or individual main with rice.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, battered or breaded, fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15209) adapted to plain deep-fried fish without batter; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asian fried fish
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact Thai-style plain fried fish entry found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried fish fillet (plain, no batter) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA composition data for deep-fried marine fish. Serving size of 180 g (6 pieces × ~30 g each) estimated from typical Thai restaurant portion. Per-100 g values reflect plain deep-fried fish without heavy batter coating, consistent with Thai pla tod preparation style.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — Atkinson et al. 2008; analogous estimate for plain fried fish based on low-carbohydrate protein-dominant food category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Thai pla tod in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 40 based on analogous plain fried fish entries (low-carbohydrate, high-protein foods) consistent with Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) which documents GI values for fried fish products in the low range (GI 38–45). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 5.04) / 100 ≈ 2.0 g (carbohydrate per serving: 2.8 g/100 g × 180 g = 5.04 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg/100 g) from fish sauce and salt used in seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 2–3 pieces and avoid additional dipping sauces high in sodium, as a full 6-piece serving contributes approximately 684 mg sodium (~30% of the 2300 mg daily limit).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and low carbohydrate content (~2.8 g/100 g) with high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thai fried fish has a low glycemic index and minimal carbohydrate load, making it a suitable protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments and with attention to the added fat from deep-frying.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying adds total fat and saturated fat; however, fish provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the deep-frying process increases total fat content, the underlying fish provides omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids beneficial for cardiovascular health; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size and prefer grilled or steamed fish preparations where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Fried Fish (Pla Tod / ปลาทอด) is a classic Thai dish consisting of whole small fish or fish fillets that are seasoned with salt, garlic, and fish sauce, then deep-fried in vegetable oil until the exterior is golden and crispy while the interior remains moist and tender. The dish is a staple of Thai home cooking and street food culture, commonly served as a shared side dish alongside steamed jasmine rice, papaya salad (som tam), and other Thai accompaniments. In Singapore, it is served at Thai restaurants such as Saap Saap Thai as a portion of 6 pieces. Nutritionally, plain deep-fried fish is a high-protein food (~20.8 g protein per 100 g) with moderate fat content (~11.5 g/100 g) contributed primarily by the frying oil. The carbohydrate content is low (~2.8 g/100 g), giving the dish a low glycemic index (estimated ~40) and a low glycemic load per serving (~2.0). Fish is a source of vitamin B12, vitamin D, selenium, phosphorus, and omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids. The sodium content is moderate (~380 mg/100 g) due to fish sauce and salt seasoning. Clinically, this dish is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI and high protein content, but sodium intake should be monitored by those with hypertension, and total fat intake should be considered by individuals with cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Thai fried fish?
Is fried fish good for diabetics?
What is the GI of fried fish?
How much protein in fried fish?
Is Thai fried fish high in sodium?
What is C2 fried fish at Saap Saap Thai?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai pla tod found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy plain fried fish data cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA composition data. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than low because the food type (plain deep-fried fish) is well-characterised in approved secondary sources and the adaptation is defensible.
notes: Serving size of 180 g (6 pieces × approximately 30 g each) is estimated from typical Thai restaurant portion sizes; actual piece weight may vary by restaurant and fish species used. Nutrient values reflect plain deep-fried fish without heavy batter coating, consistent with Thai pla tod style. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for fried fish; no sentinel -1 values are used as all fields are populated with defensible estimates from approved sources. GI set to 40 as an analogous estimate for plain fried fish based on Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); gi_category is low. No core macros are zero. Halal status assumed based on fish ingredient; kosher status not confirmed. Gluten-free status assumed for plain preparation without batter; restaurant-specific cross-contamination risk not assessed.

Added FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology anchors with closest_parent match type and gap notes; SNOMED CT set to none with gap note.
Added all three required health context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
